coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots with tunable third-order nonlinear coefficient instructed by machine learning
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xiao
Wang, Hongshuai
Zhou, Wenfa
Zhang, Tianyang
Huang, Hui
Song, Yinglin
Li, Youyong
Liu, Yang
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: Nonlinear optical materials are important in community of telecommunication, light-information storage and photo-dynamic therapy. Here, we show a series of carbon dots (CDs, CDs-1 to CDs-72) fabricated from oxalamide with tunable third-order nonlinear optics (NLO) susceptibility χ(3) and switching behavior. These CDs exhibit a size-dependent effect on third-order nonlinear refractive (NLR) index γ and third-order nonlinear saturable absorption (NLSA) coefficient β. Also. they show the NLO switching behavior as the incident laser energy increasing. With the instruction of machine learning, CDs with tunable χ(3) from 0 to 1.79 × 10−8 esu were synthesized as expected. Among them, CDs-12 show NLRSA behavior with third-order NLO susceptibility χ(3) of 3.59 × 10−10 esu, CDs-24 exhibit NLSA behavior with χ(3) of 3.03 × 10−10 esu and CDs-36 own the best susceptibility χ(3) of 1.79 × 10−8 esu with NLO switching behavior.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Machine Learning
Third-order nonlinear refractive
Third-order nonlinear saturable absorption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Third-order nonlinear optical response of CDs
Machine learning instructing an adjustable third-order nonlinear susceptibility coefficient χ(3)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterizations of CDs. TEM image of CDs with reaction time for a, 12 h and b, 24 h, inserted images are the corresponding HRTEM images, respectively. c, XRD pattern. d, UV–vis spectra. e, FTIR spectra and f, Raman spectra of CDs with reaction for 12 h (black solid line) and 24 h (red solid line).
Bandgap structure of CDs. The curve of hν vs (αhν)1/2 for a, CDs-12 and b, CDs-24. CV curve of c, CDs-12 and d, CDs-24.

Z-scan results of CDs. a and c, open aperture z-scan results of CDs-12 and CDs-24. b and d, closed aperture results of CDs-12 and CDs-24. Z-scan was equipped with 532 nm picosecond (15 ps) pulsed laser.
The schematic of machine learning instructing the CDs synthesized.

Characterizations of machine learning models. a, boxplots of XGB, GBDT, SVM and RF. b, descriptor importance deduced from training data of GBDT model. c, curves of training data (red dot line) and test data (blue dot line) for GBDT model. d, the heat map of descriptor correlation among the selected descriptor.
Machine learning instructing the adjustable third-order NLO responses. Open aperture Z-scan results of a, CDs-8; b, CDs-16; c, CDs-23; d, CDs-36.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots with tunable third-order nonlinear coefficient instructed by machine learning

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, 199 Ren'ai Road, Suzhou 215123, China (Y. Li and Z. Kang).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)
Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The process of machine learning instructing carbon dots with tunable third-order nonlinear coefficient.

Nonlinear optical materials are important in community of telecommunication, light-information storage and photo-dynamic therapy. Here, we show a series of carbon dots (CDs, CDs-1 to CDs-72) fabricated from oxalamide with tunable third-order nonlinear optics (NLO) susceptibility χ(3) and switching behavior. These CDs exhibit a size-dependent effect on third-order nonlinear refractive (NLR) index γ and third-order nonlinear saturable absorption (NLSA) coefficient β. Also. they show the NLO switching behavior as the incident laser energy increasing. With the instruction of machine learning, CDs with tunable χ(3) from 0 to 1.79 × 10−8 esu were synthesized as expected. Among them, CDs-12 show NLRSA behavior with third-order NLO susceptibility χ(3) of 3.59 × 10−10 esu, CDs-24 exhibit NLSA behavior with χ(3) of 3.03 × 10−10 esu and CDs-36 own the best susceptibility χ(3) of 1.79 × 10−8 esu with NLO switching behavior.

ce:section.ce:para: Nonlinear optics (NLO) has gradually shown its essential importance in laser radiation protection, optical limiting and optical switching.[1–12] And NLO materials are necessary in community of telecommunication, light-information storage and photo-dynamic therapy, etc.[13–25] Carbon dots (CDs) are low cost, non-toxic and eco-friendly, which have been widely applied in biology, pharmaceutics, bioimaging, catalysis and sensing.[26–29] What is more, CDs are an outstanding volunteer to be applied for NLO materials since its inherent properties.[30] Recently, CDs have been developed to study their third-order NLO by nano/pico-second laser pulses with Z-scan. It is found that CDs have NLO propertise that NLO refractive (NLR) index reduced and absorption coefficient increased with the size increasing.[31] Functional groups on the surface of CDs also do an important effect on the third-order NLO susceptibility.[32] In addition, doping elements CDs were found to have a faster NLO response than CDs without doping elements.[31] Moreover, CDs were observed to have a third-order NLO switching behavior from NLO saturable absorption to reverse absorption as the laser energy increased which would be essential to optical limiting.[33] While, many factors which have not been investigated clearly, such as, composition, structure, surface functional groups, conjugation domain, and size will strongly affect the NLO properties of CDs.

In our previous work, machine learning was used to instruct an important parameter (correlated color temperature) in CDs based WLEDs to remove extra and long efforts.[27] Lin et al, employed machine learning to investigate the second harmonic generation coefficients of nonlinear optical crystals with distinct diamond-like structures, showing promising applications to explore new crystal materials of quaternary DL system with superior mid-IR NLO performances.[34] CCSD calculations comprise the most complete and accurate calculations of linear polarizabilities and second hyperpolarizabilites.[35] Nevertheless, there is still no work that refers to making use of machine learning to instruct the tunable third-order NLO susceptibility χ(3) and switching behavior. Here, we synthesized CDs through a hydrothermal method with oxalamide dispersed in ultrapure water. The properties of third-order NLO were detected by Z-scan technique equipped with a picosecond pulsed laser. These CDs show unexceptionable third-order NLO performances with tunable third-order NLO susceptibility χ(3) and switching behavior. Third-order NLO based on CDs show a size-dependent on third-order nonlinear refractive (NLR) index γ and third-order nonlinear saturable absorption (NLSA) coefficient β. CDs-12 show third-order nonlinear reverse saturable absorption (NLRSA) behavior with third-order NLO susceptibility χ(3) of 3.59 × 10−10 esu and CDs-24 exhibit NLSA behavior with χ(3) of 3.03 × 10−10 esu. Then, with instruction of machine learning, CDs with tunable χ(3) from 0 to 1.79 × 10−8 esu (CDs-36) were synthesized. Also, these CDs exhibit the NLO switching behavior with the incident laser energy increasing, which make it a promising candidate for optical limiting.
The chemicals and reagents used in these experiments are purchased from Sinopharm Group with no purified steps.

The CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method. Briefly, the oxalamide was dispersed in 40–80 mL ultrapure water (pure water, deionized water or distilled water) with 200–400 °C for different reaction time. Then the resulted solution was centrifuged for 5 min to remove the precipitate including precursor and other outgrowth, repeating this step for three times. The supernate containing CDs and organic molecule was dialyzed with cellulose dialysis membrane (500–1000 Da) using ultrapure water as dialysate. Finally, the dried CDs powders were obtained via rotary evaporation. The CDs prepared with reaction time for 1, 2, 3, …, 71, 72 h with reaction temperature from 200 to 400 °C, so they were named as formation of CDs-reaction time-reaction temperature (CDs-1-200, CDs-2-200, CDs-3, …, CDs-71-400, CDs-72-400), respectively. CDs with reaction temperature of 400 °C were the most used in this work, thus they were simplified as CDs-reaction time (CDs-1, CDs-2, CDs-3, …, CDs-71, CDs-72).

Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) test were carried out with a standard three-electrode system, including working, counter and reference electrodes. The mixed solution of ethanol (95%) and Nafion containing 10 mg CDs were made into a homogeneous solution so that CDs can be dropped onto the surface of working electrode. The N2 was performed to clean up the O2 or H2O in the mixed solution. The ferrocene was used as the interior label of potential. We use scan speed of 50 mV s−1 to collect the experimental data.

The machine learning models (RF: random forest, XGB: XGBoost, SVM: support vector machine and GBDT: gradient boosting decision tree) compared in the text were acquired from scikit-learn. GBDT is a representative algorithm in the series of algorithms. It is an iterative decision tree algorithm, which is composed of multiple decision trees, and the final conclusion was made up of each decision tree. RF is a forest built in a random way. The forest is made up of many decision trees, in which there is no correlation between each decision. SVM is a dichotomous model, which aims to separate the two part from eigenvectors in space in the best way. XGB is a hoisting tree model, which integrates many tree models together to form a strong classifier. GBDT and XGB are subclasses of decision tree models. Assuming that each node of a tree corresponds to a dataset, the decision tree will split the existing datasets to multiple subsets by judging whether the eigenvalues of the samples meet specific conditions, in order to reduce the internal difference of each subset as much as possible. With the goal of minimizing a loss function, GBDT gives prediction based on multiple decision trees, and the loss function is minimized by Gradient Boosting Algorithm, which accelerates the optimization of the model. Based on second-order expansion and regularization, XGB improves the loss function and optimization process of GBDT, and thus improves the generality and suppresses over fitting. While SVM and MLP are ML algorithms based on constructing hyperplanes. By minimizing a loss function, the hyperplanes can optimally separate or approximate the data points (corresponding to samples) in the feature space, so as to make classification and regression. SVM is characterized by hinge/ε-insensitive loss function, and the idea of maximum/minimum margin. Combined with kernel method, SVM can also handle nonlinear analysis. MLP can be easily customized by setting the number of nodes/layers, and is compatible with more available loss functions. Unlike SVM, MLP makes nonlinear analysis by multi-layer linear transformation and nonlinear activation function, which is easier to be realized than finding appropriate kernel functions. Generally, SVM and MLP are more applicable under the situations of high-dimensional features and big data, while GDBT and XGB perform better and faster under the opposite situation.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained through FEI TF20 transmission electron microscope. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) spectra were measured by PIXcel3D x-ray diffractometer. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were collected from lambda UV spectrometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were tested by Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Raman spectra were fetched via HR 800 Raman spectroscope. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) spectra were detected by Hyperion spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) spectra were conducted by KRATOS Axis ultra-DLD x-ray photoelectron spectroscope. CV curves were graphed by a standard three-electron configuration with CHI760E workstation.

Fig. 1 a and 1b depict TEM images of CDs dispersed in ultrapure water. It can be seen that both CDs display well dispersibility in size. And the size distributions of CDs-12 (CDs with reaction time for 12 h) and CDs-24 (CDs with reaction time for 24 h) are shown in Fig. S1a and S1b. The average sizes of CDs-12 and CDs-24 are 3.03 nm and 4.20 nm, severally. Inset in Fig. 1a and 1b are the high resolution TEM images for both CDs, exhibiting lattice spacing of 0.21 nm which is attributed to graphite plane of (100). XRD patterns of both CDs in Fig. 1c depict broad peaks at the position of 21° in line with the graphite (002) lattice.

UV–vis spectra were measured in Fig. 1d to analysis electron transition way. Both CDs manifest a strong peak at 270 nm corresponding to π-π* transition of carbon–carbon double bond and a should peak at 330 nm resulting from n-π* transition of amino. PL peaks for both CDs are located at 460 nm under the excitation spectra of 410 nm (Fig. S1c). Fig. 1e shows the FTIR spectra of both CDs. They both describe O–H (3441 cm−1), N–H (3186 cm−1), C–H (2916 cm−1), C=O (1632 cm−1), C-N (1389 cm−1) and C-O (1070 cm−1) existing on the surface of CDs. Raman spectra (Fig. 1f) were detected to characterize the degree of carbonization for CDs. Both CDs own D band (1320 cm−1) and G band (1630 cm−1). With different reaction time, the IG/ID for CDs-12 is 1.37 and IG/ID for CDs-24 is 0.97.

XPS survey spectra in Fig. S2a and S2e exhibit both CDs contain C (285.36 eV), O (534.88 eV) and N (401.09 eV) elements. The high resolution XPS C 1s spectra in Fig. S2b for CDs-12 can be divided into three peaks: C=O (287.56 eV), C-O/C-N (285.79 eV) and C=C/C–C (284.49 eV). The high resolution XPS O 1s in Fig. S2c for CDs-12 can be grouped into three peaks: C-O (533.51 eV), C=O (531.75 eV) and COO (530.66 eV). The high-resolution N 1s spectra in Fig. S2d for CDs-12 can be identified into three peaks: graphitic N (401.42 eV), pyrrolic N (400.23 eV) and pyridinic N (398.87 eV). The high resolution XPS C 1s spectra in Fig. S2f for CDs-24 can be divided into three peaks: C=O (287.56 eV), C-O/C-N (285.60 eV) and C=C/C–C (284.46 eV). The high resolution XPS O 1s spectra in Fig. S2g for CDs-24 can be grouped into three peaks: C-O (533.36 eV), C=O (531.77 eV) and COO (530.55 eV). The high-resolution N 1s spectra in Fig. S2h for CDs-24 can be identified into three peaks: graphitic N (401.60 eV), pyrrolic N (400.39 eV) and pyridinic N (398.97 eV).

The bandgap structure of CDs is calculated by both optical and CV method. Inset in Fig. 2 a show the derived picture from UV–vis spectra. The hν = 1240/λ (abscissa axis) and (αhν)1/2 (vertical axis) were carried out to graph the curve. The crosspoint between abscissa axis and tangent for the onset peak in curve was the bandgap energy (Eg) value. Thus, the bandgap structure of CDs-12 can be calculated as: Eg = 2.27 eV. The bandgap structure of CDs-24 can be calculated as: Eg = 2.21 eV. Then, the CV method was used to verify the data of optical method. It can be summarized as follows: A graphite rod was used as counter electrode, Ag/AgCl was used as reference electrode, a glass carbon electrode was used as working electrode and ferrocene was used as internal standard. With a 10 mV/s scan speed, the bandgap structure can be deduced from the oxidation and reduction potential and the bandgap of CDs-12 and CDs-24 can be calculated to be 2.29 and 2.18 eV, whose data are corresponding to optical method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Third-order nonlinear optical response of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Third-order nonlinear optical properties of CDs were investigated by Z-scan measurement appending a 532 nm picosecond (15 ps) pulsed laser. The detailed information about Z-scan measurements can be seen in section of Z-scan measurement in supporting information. Fig. 3 a depicts the open aperture Z-scan results of CDs-12. There is an obvious and narrow transmission valley, indicating a prominent third-order NLRSA response. CDs-24 in Fig. 3b shows a significant transmission peak, implying a remarkable third-order NLSA response. Fig. 3c and 3d exhibit the closed aperture Z-scan results of CDs-12 and CDs-24. Two pictures both display a trend of after the peak valley first, demonstrating the third-order nonlinear positive refraction response of both CDs. Meanwhile, the key parameters of third-order NLO (third-order NLO susceptibility coefficient χ(3), third-order NLSA coefficient β and third-order NLR coefficient γ) for both has been calculated to further investigate the principle of CDs based third-order NLO performances. β was deduced from the fitted curves for NLRSA and NLSA curves. γ was deduced from the fitted curves for third-order NLR curves. Then, the χ(3) was calculated from the above coefficients and corresponding calculated process can be seen in section of Z-scan measurement in supporting information. β, γ, and χ(3) of CDs-12 can be deduced to 1.03 × 10−12 m/W, 8.18 × 10−18 m2/W, and 3.59 × 10−10 esu. β, γ, and χ(3) of CDs-24 can be deduced to 9.81 × 10−16 m/W, 6.92 × 10−18 m2/W, and 3.03 × 10−10 esu.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Machine learning instructing an adjustable third-order nonlinear susceptibility coefficient χ(3)
ce:section.ce:para: Although the CDs with NLSA and NLRSA behavior have been synthesized by hydrothermal method, yet this process was very complicated and disordered with too many reaction conditions being taken into consideration. It has always been a big challenging, which hinders the evolution of CDs synthesizing. So, machine learning, a state of art technique, pointing its purpose on finding the various correlations among different thing with computer is coming to take insight on the synthesized process of CDs. The machine learning schematic can be seen in Fig. 4 and the detailed information about machine learning will be seen in the experimental section. Briefly, input features and output target should be selected and chosen from the perspective of experiment. Then, the optimized machine learning model should be selected among all the algorithm. At last, the optimized model will optimize and predict the results, which will be compared with actual experimental results.

Since the third-order NLSA, NLRSA and third-order NLO susceptibility coefficient χ(3) transformed with two different CDs, we think that there will be a way to instruct these transformation. Therefore, machine learning shall be an appropriate method to perfectly deal with that in a perfect way. Seven corresponding input features referring to synthesized process of CDs have been introduced into machine learning calculated process, containing T (℃; reaction temperature), t (h; reaction time), V (reaction filling degree), m (g; mass of oxalamide), v (μL; volume of concentrated sulfuric acid), r (℃/min; heating rate) and w (pure water, ultrapure water, deionized water or distilled water) with very low correlation to each other. 200 different kinds of experiments with various experimental reaction conditions were done and values of χ(3) were calculated to appeal to the machine learning models. In them, 180 data were used to be training data and the others were performed to be test data. Then, the four classical regression models (RF, XGB, SVM and GBDT) was used in this machine learning study.

Fig. 5 a compares evaluated results of four machine learning models in the form of boxplot. It expresses that GBDT dominates other three machine learning models and was chosen as the most appropriate model to instruct the values of χ(3). For in detail learning the pivotal role from synthesized methods of CDs to χ(3) in terms of experimental data, feature importance which was an important index to machine learning was selected in trained GBDT model and had been graphed in Fig. 5b. This image means the reaction time is the most important descriptor to determine the value of χ(3), following with the reaction temperature and reaction filling degree in queue. The GBDT model in Fig. 5c displays the curves of training data and test data owning Rtrain of 91% and Rtest of 97%, indicating the big success of GBDT model to predict the inferred results. Other machine models (XGB: Rtrain of 87% and Rtest of 96%; SVM: Rtrain of 69% and Rtest of 67%; RF: Rtrain of 40% and Rtest of 68%) to predict the χ(3) are shown in Fig. S3 and the detailed data of R and RMSE to evaluate the four machine learning model are shown in Table S1. The main hyperparameters of SVM model is gamma (0.047) and C (45), and the kernel of the SVM model is ‘rbf’. The RF, GBDT, and XGB algorithm are the Decision Tree models. The hyperparameters of these models include ‘n_estimators’,’ min_samples_split’, ‘max_depth’,’ min_samples_leaf’, ‘learning_rate’, and ‘max_features’, which are summarized in Table S2. Also, the correlation of each feature is exhibited in Fig. 5d. The heat map tells the little correlation among the seven descriptors. In all, the results of GBDT model are in consistent with our expectation.

With help of machine learning, the work efficiency will be raised by big percentages. Under assistance of feature importance, all the reaction conditions are optimized to the reaction time and reaction temperature which are the first two important factor to identify the experimental results in a specific condition. Also, the GBDT was used to predict the χ(3) with the computer itself without supervision from resarchers, meaning easing people for “free”. However, it still needs people to do realistic experiment to verify the validation of artificial intelligence. Some specific results are displayed in Table S3-S4. It can be seen that the predicted and experimental values keep the same at counting units while there is hardly any error. So, by adjusting reaction time with the reaction temperature at 400 °C, the third-order NLO responses have been adjusted from third-order NLRSA to NLSA response and the third-order NLO susceptibility coefficient χ(3) has been adjusted from 0 to 1.79 × 10−8 esu.

The specific third-order NLO behavior of CDs instructed by machine learning will be discussed in the following section. Fig. 6 a-6d demonstrate that third-order NLO responses from NLRSA to NLSA which are seldom found in previous experiment have been proved to be existed. When the reaction time is less than 4 h (Fig. S4a), there is hardly third-order NLO responses been found. But with the reaction time is up to 8 h (Fig. S4b), very small third-order NLRSA behavior appeared. As the time going on, the NLRSA behavior gradually increased (Fig. S4a-S4d). At the reaction time of 16 h, the NLRSA show its deepest transmission valley. The NLRSA gradually decreased to zero with the reaction up to 20 h (Fig. S4d-S4f). Then, in reaction time between 19 and 24 h, CDs show third-order NLSA and increased with the reaction time passing (Fig. S5a-S5e). Reaction time of 36 h shows the highest third-order NLSA response (Fig. S5e). Reaction time of 48 h (Fig. S5f) shows that the third-order NLO response begin to decrease, too. Fig. S6a-S6c depict that the third-order NLSA gradually decrease to zero. At the same time, third-order NLO susceptibility coefficient χ(3) exhibits the same variation trend with variation trend of third-order NLO response. More detailed reaction conditions and exact values corresponding to values of χ(3) can be seen in Table S3.

Also, third-order NLO switching behavior of the same CDs has been investigated in Fig. S7a-S7e. It can be seen that CDs-36 show gradually fade trend when input energy increases from 0.6 to 1.4 μj, but they show an opposite trend while input energy increases from 1.4 to 2.4 μj. The principle of NLO switching behavior can be explained by our previous work. [33] When incident laser energy is low, the electron transits from ground state to first excited state, whose process leads to NLSA behavior. As the incident laser energy sustainably increases to 1.4 μj, electron appears to transit through excited states, resulting to NLRSA behavior.

In summary, we have synthesized a series of CDs by a simple hydrothermal method, which have adjustable third-order NLO susceptibility coefficient χ(3) and third-order NLO switching responses. Machine learning was applied to find the variation principle of third-order NLO susceptibility coefficient χ(3) from 200 experimental data with much high work efficiency. For these CDs, the optimized NLO susceptibility coefficient χ(3) instructed by GBDT model is 1.79 × 10−8 esu (CDs-36). Also, third-order NLO switching behavior has been found for the same sample of CDs-36, as the laser energy increased from 1.4 to 2.4 μj. Furthermore, machine learning will set researchers for free in the period of investigating the variation principle of third-order NLO susceptibility coefficient χ(3) from 200 experimental data which also save lots of time. Then the accurate predictions will also avoid consuming time on repeated and boring experiment. Moreover, this machine learning model can provide convenience for later researchers who show interest on the third-order NLO properties about CDs.

Xiao Wang: Data curation, Visualization. Hongshuai Wang: Formal analysis. Wenfa Zhou: Investigation. Tianyang Zhang: Software. Hui Huang: Methodology. Yinglin Song: Writing – original draft, Supervision. Youyong Li: Writing – original draft, Supervision. Yang Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Zhenhui Kang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This work is supported by National Key R&D Program of China (2020YFA0406104, 2020YFA0406101, 2020YFA0406103), National MCF Energy R&D Program (2018YFE0306105), Innovative Research Group Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51821002), National Natural Science Foundation of China (51725204, 21771132, 51972216, 52041202‬), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20190041), Key-Area Research and Development Program of GuangDong Province (2019B010933001), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology, the 111 Project and Suzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nano & Soft Materials.
The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2021.113729.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Growth and investigations of 3rd order NLO properties of novel semi organic tartaric acid lithium sulfate single crystal for photonics application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Rajeswari, G. Palani, B. Deepanraj, P.L. Biju, V. Chithambaram, Growth and investigations of 3rd order NLO properties of novel semi organic tartaric acid lithium sulfate single crystal for photonics application, Opt. Quant. Electron. 52 (2020) 367. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11082-020-02481-8.
